author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Written in the form of a letter addressed to the Catholics, in which the first portion of the letter which Petilian had written to his adherents is examined and refuted.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In conclusion, the Testament is said to have been given to the flames by certain men in the time of persecution.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Now let its lessons be read, from whatever source it has been brought to light.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Certainly in the beginning of the promises of the Testator this is found to have been said to Abraham: "In thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed;" and this saying is truthfully interpreted by the apostle: "To thy seed," he says, "which is Christ."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: No betrayal on the part of any man has made the promises of God of none effect.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Hold communion with all the nations of the earth, and then you may boast that you have preserved the Testament from the destruction of the flames.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you will not do so, which party is the rather to be believed to have insisted on the burning of the Testament, save that which will not assent to its teaching when it is brought to light?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For how much more certainly, without any sacrilegious rashness, can he be held to have joined the company of traditors who now persecutes with his tongue the Testament which they are said to have persecuted with the flames!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You charge us with the persecution: the true wheat of the Lord answers you, "Either it was done justly, or it was done by the chaff that was among us."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What have you to say to this?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You object that we have no baptism: the same true wheat of the Lord answers you, that the form of the sacrament even within the Church fails to profit some, as it did no good to Simon Magus when he was baptized, much more it fails to profit those who are without.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Yet that baptism remains in them when they depart, is proved from this, that it is not restored to them when they return.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Never, therefore, except by the greatest shamelessness, will you be able to cry out against that wheat, or to call them false prophets clad in sheep’s clothing, whilst inwardly they are ravening wolves; since either they do not know the wicked in the unity of the Catholic Church, or for the sake of unity bear with those whom they know.
